name: The Alamo
clue: This 18th-century Spanish mission in downtown San Antonio became the site of a legendary 1836 battle during the Texas Revolution, where a small garrison was overwhelmed after a 13-day siege.
funFact: The defenders held out for 13 days against an army estimated at over 1,800 soldiers before being overrun.

name: Big Bend National Park
clue: Named for the sharp turn the Rio Grande makes along its southern border, this West Texas national park contains an entire mountain range rising from the Chihuahuan Desert.
funFact: It has more species of birds than any other national park in the United States.

name: Texas State Capitol
clue: Built from sunset red granite, this building in Austin is taller than the US Capitol in Washington D.C. and has served as the seat of Texas government since 1888.
funFact: The dome is 14 feet taller than the US Capitol dome.

name: Guadalupe Mountains National Park
clue: This West Texas park contains the highest peak in the state, and its dramatic limestone reef was formed from an ancient marine fossil reef roughly 265 million years ago.
funFact: Guadalupe Peak at 8,749 feet is the highest point in Texas.

name: Palo Duro Canyon
clue: Often called the 'Grand Canyon of Texas', this dramatic canyon in the Texas Panhandle is the second largest canyon in the United States, carved by a fork of the Red River.
funFact: The canyon walls expose rock layers spanning over 250 million years of geological history.

name: San Jacinto Monument
clue: Standing near Houston, this obelisk marks the site of the 1836 battle that lasted just 18 minutes and secured Texas independence from Mexico. It is the world's tallest masonry column.
funFact: The battle of San Jacinto lasted only 18 minutes, making it one of the shortest decisive battles in history.

name: Enchanted Rock
clue: This massive pink granite dome rising 425 feet above the Hill Country floor has been sacred to Native Americans for thousands of years and creaks and groans at night as it cools.
funFact: The creaking sounds at night led Native Americans to believe the rock was haunted or enchanted.

name: Johnson Space Center
clue: Located in Houston, this NASA facility has served as the mission control center for every US human spaceflight since Gemini 4 in 1965, including the Apollo moon landings.
funFact: The phrase 'Houston, we have a problem' was spoken to this facility during the Apollo 13 mission in 1970.

name: Padre Island National Seashore
clue: This barrier island along the Gulf Coast is the longest undeveloped barrier island in the world and serves as critical nesting habitat for endangered Kemp's ridley sea turtles.
funFact: At 70 miles long, it is the longest stretch of undeveloped barrier island on Earth.

name: Dealey Plaza
clue: This small urban park in downtown Dallas became one of the most infamous locations in American history on November 22, 1963, and is now home to a museum dedicated to that day.
funFact: The Sixth Floor Museum at the former Texas School Book Depository receives over 300,000 visitors per year.

name: Caddo Lake
clue: This mysterious cypress swamp on the Texas-Louisiana border is the only natural lake in Texas and is draped in Spanish moss, creating an atmosphere unlike anywhere else in the state.
funFact: It is the only large natural lake in Texas; all others were created by dams.

name: Austin
clue: This Texas capital along the Colorado River bills itself as the 'Live Music Capital of the World' and hosts one of the largest music and film festivals in the country each spring.
funFact: Austin has more live music venues per capita than Nashville.

name: San Antonio
clue: This South Texas city is home to a famous river walk lined with restaurants and shops, five Spanish colonial missions designated as a UNESCO World Heritage Site, and the most visited site in the state.
funFact: The San Antonio River Walk sits about 20 feet below street level and stretches 15 miles.

name: Houston
clue: The largest city in Texas and the fourth largest in the US, this Gulf Coast metropolis is the energy capital of the world, home to more Fortune 500 companies than any city except New York.
funFact: Houston has no zoning laws, making it unique among major American cities.

name: Galveston
clue: This barrier island city on the Gulf Coast was once the most populous city in Texas and was devastated by the deadliest natural disaster in US history — a hurricane in 1900.
funFact: The 1900 Galveston hurricane killed an estimated 6,000–12,000 people, still the deadliest natural disaster in US history.

name: El Paso
clue: Nestled in the far western tip of Texas where three states and two countries meet, this desert city sits across the Rio Grande from a major Mexican city and shares the same metropolitan area.
funFact: El Paso and Ciudad Juárez form the largest binational metropolitan area on the US-Mexico border.

name: Cadillac Ranch
clue: Just west of Amarillo along Route 66, this public art installation features ten Cadillacs buried nose-first in a flat Texas Panhandle field, all spray-painted by visitors.
funFact: The cars are angled to match the slope of the Great Pyramid of Giza.

name: Corpus Christi
clue: This South Texas coastal city on a large natural bay is home to the USS Lexington aircraft carrier museum and serves as the gateway to Padre Island National Seashore.
funFact: It is one of the largest ports in the United States by cargo tonnage.

name: Hamilton Pool Preserve
clue: This stunning natural swimming hole west of Austin was formed when the dome of an underground river collapsed thousands of years ago, creating a grotto with a 50-foot waterfall.
funFact: The pool was created when the roof of an underground river collapsed due to massive erosion.

name: Fort Worth Stockyards
clue: This historic district in Fort Worth preserves the legacy of the cattle trade that once made this city the last stop before the Western frontier, with twice-daily cattle drives still held on its cobblestone streets.
funFact: Fort Worth was once the largest cattle market in the world.

name: Dallas
clue: The third-largest city in Texas is known for its gleaming skyline, upscale shopping, and a thriving arts district that is one of the largest contiguous urban arts districts in the United States.
funFact: The Dallas Arts District is the largest contiguous urban arts district in the nation at 68 acres.

name: Fort Worth
clue: Known as 'Cowtown', this city sits where the American West truly begins and pairs a world-class museum district — including one of the finest art museums in the South — with genuine rodeo culture.
funFact: Fort Worth's Kimbell Art Museum is considered one of the finest small art museums in the world.

name: Marfa
clue: This tiny West Texas desert town of fewer than 2,000 people became an unlikely art world destination after a minimalist sculptor moved there in the 1970s and filled a former army base with permanent large-scale installations.
funFact: The Marfa Lights, unexplained glowing orbs seen on the horizon at night, have mystified observers for over a century.

name: Fredericksburg
clue: Founded by German immigrants in 1846, this Hill Country town still reflects its heritage in its architecture and cuisine, and sits at the center of a thriving wine region with over 50 wineries nearby.
funFact: The National Museum of the Pacific War, honoring Fleet Admiral Chester Nimitz who was born here, is located in this small Texas town.

name: Waco
clue: This Central Texas city on the Brazos River is home to the world's largest collection of Baylor University and gained nationwide fame when a pair of TV personalities turned a run-down building into a home renovation empire.
funFact: The Waco Mammoth National Monument contains the only recorded evidence of a nursery herd of Columbian mammoths in the US.

name: Lubbock
clue: Rising from the flat South Plains of West Texas, this city is the birthplace of rock and roll pioneer Buddy Holly and sits at the heart of the largest contiguous cotton-growing region in the world.
funFact: The region around Lubbock produces roughly 25% of all US cotton.

name: Abilene
clue: Once the end of the legendary Chisholm Trail cattle drives in the 1870s, this West Texas city reinvented itself as an educational and military hub and is home to Dyess Air Force Base.
funFact: The Abilene State Park preserves a rare remnant of the original Texas buffalo range.

name: San Angelo
clue: Nestled where three rivers converge in West Texas, this city is known as the 'Wool and Mohair Capital of the World' and contains a beautifully restored frontier-era fort along the Concho River.
funFact: San Angelo produces more wool and mohair than any other region in the United States.

name: Laredo
clue: Sitting directly on the Rio Grande across from its Mexican sister city, this South Texas border town is the largest inland port on the US-Mexico border and one of the oldest communities in Texas.
funFact: Laredo is the largest inland port of entry in the United States, handling over 40% of US-Mexico land trade.

name: Brownsville
clue: The southernmost city in Texas sits at the mouth of the Rio Grande and was the site of the first battle of the Mexican-American War in 1846. Today its wildlife corridor hosts some of the rarest birds in North America.
funFact: The area around Brownsville is one of the top birdwatching destinations in the US, home to over 500 species.

name: Lockhart
clue: The Texas Legislature officially declared this small town south of Austin the 'BBQ Capital of Texas', home to several legendary smokehouse institutions that have been slow-cooking brisket for generations.
funFact: Kreuz Market in Lockhart has been smoking meat since 1900 and famously serves no sauce — the meat speaks for itself.

name: Natural Bridge Caverns
clue: Named for the 60-foot natural limestone bridge spanning its entrance, this cave system near San Antonio is the largest commercially operated cavern in Texas and contains spectacular stalactite and stalagmite formations.
funFact: The caverns maintain a constant temperature of 70°F year-round regardless of outside conditions.

name: Lost Maples State Natural Area
clue: Tucked into a remote canyon of the Texas Hill Country, this preserve shelters a rare stand of Uvalde bigtooth maple trees that turn brilliant red and orange each fall — unusual for subtropical Texas.
funFact: These maple trees are a relict population isolated from other maple forests by hundreds of miles.

name: Pedernales Falls State Park
clue: Just west of Austin, the Pedernales River cascades over broad limestone steps in this Hill Country park, near the ranch where a Texas-born US President spent much of his presidency.
funFact: President Lyndon B. Johnson's LBJ Ranch is just a few miles away and is now a National Historical Park.

name: Barton Springs Pool
clue: Fed by underground springs that maintain a constant 68°F temperature year-round, this three-acre natural swimming pool in the heart of Austin has been a gathering place for locals for over a century.
funFact: The spring-fed pool is home to the endangered Barton Springs salamander, found nowhere else on Earth.

name: Dinosaur Valley State Park
clue: The bed of the Paluxy River in Glen Rose exposes some of the best-preserved dinosaur trackways in the world, where massive sauropod and theropod footprints were pressed into mud over 110 million years ago.
funFact: The tracks include both plant-eating sauropods and predatory theropods, offering a rare snapshot of dinosaur interaction.

name: Hueco Tanks State Park
clue: Rising from the Chihuahuan Desert near El Paso, these rocky hills contain natural rock basins that collected rainwater, making them a vital oasis for humans for over 10,000 years and leaving behind thousands of pictographs.
funFact: The site contains over 3,000 pictographs representing cultures spanning 10,000 years, including rare 'Jornada Mogollon' mask paintings.

name: Balmorhea State Park
clue: In the desert of West Texas, this park contains the world's largest spring-fed swimming pool — over 1.75 acres filled by San Solomon Springs, which pushes out 22–26 million gallons of 72–76°F water daily.
funFact: The pool is deep enough for scuba diving and is home to two endangered fish species found only in this spring system.

name: Seminole Canyon State Park
clue: This dramatic canyon in southwest Texas shelters some of North America's oldest rock art — pictographs painted by ancient peoples over 4,000 years ago — deep inside a rugged desert gorge above the Pecos River.
funFact: The Fate Bell Shelter contains pictographs estimated to be 4,000 years old, among the oldest rock art in North America.

name: Washington-on-the-Brazos
clue: On March 2, 1836, delegates gathered at this small town on the Brazos River to sign the Texas Declaration of Independence, making it the birthplace of the Republic of Texas.
funFact: Texas Independence Day is still celebrated on March 2nd, the date the declaration was signed here in 1836.

name: Goliad State Park & Historic Site
clue: At this South Texas mission complex, 342 Texan prisoners of war were executed on Palm Sunday 1836 on orders from Santa Anna — an event that galvanized Texan forces and became a battle cry alongside the Alamo.
funFact: 'Remember Goliad' became a rallying cry just like 'Remember the Alamo' after the massacre of Colonel Fannin's men.

name: Fort Davis National Historic Site
clue: Perched in the Davis Mountains of West Texas, this frontier army post was established to protect travelers on the San Antonio-El Paso Road and was famously garrisoned by the Buffalo Soldiers after the Civil War.
funFact: Fort Davis is one of the best-preserved frontier military posts in the American Southwest.

name: McDonald Observatory
clue: Perched atop Mount Locke in the Davis Mountains, this research observatory benefits from some of the darkest skies in the continental United States and hosts public star parties that draw visitors from across the country.
funFact: The area around McDonald Observatory has some of the least light pollution of any region in the lower 48 states.

name: Gruene Hall
clue: Built in 1878 in the tiny Guadalupe River town of Gruene, this is the oldest continuously operating dance hall in Texas, where legends like Willie Nelson and Lyle Lovett have played on a humble stage.
funFact: Gruene Hall has no air conditioning — just open windows and ceiling fans — and has operated continuously since 1878.

name: Battleship Texas
clue: Moored near the San Jacinto Monument outside Houston, this dreadnought battleship is the last surviving warship to have served in both World Wars and was the first US Navy ship to be preserved as a museum.
funFact: The USS Texas supported the D-Day landings at Normandy in 1944 and earned five battle stars in World War II.

name: South Padre Island
clue: At the southernmost tip of Texas, this narrow barrier island resort town on the Gulf of Mexico is a spring break destination and home to Sea Turtle, Inc., a rehabilitation center for injured sea turtles.
funFact: South Padre Island is one of the top spring break destinations in the US, drawing over 100,000 college students annually.

name: Luckenbach
clue: This unincorporated Hill Country community consists of little more than a general store, a dance hall, and a post office, yet became immortalized in a 1977 country song and draws thousands of visitors who come to do absolutely nothing.
funFact: Luckenbach has a permanent population of about 3 people but draws over 250,000 visitors per year.

name: Port Aransas
clue: Accessible only by ferry or a long bridge, this laid-back fishing village on Mustang Island is the saltwater fishing capital of Texas and hosts a remarkable spring bird migration with over 400 species recorded.
funFact: Port Aransas is considered one of the top birding destinations in the United States during spring migration.

name: LBJ Ranch
clue: Known as the 'Texas White House', this working ranch in the Hill Country was the beloved home of the 36th President, who was born nearby, held cabinet meetings under live oak trees, and was buried here.
funFact: LBJ was so proud of Texas that he held foreign dignitaries and press conferences on the ranch, herding cattle while discussing policy.

name: Palo Alto Battlefield
clue: On May 8, 1846, the first major battle of the Mexican-American War was fought on this coastal prairie near present-day Brownsville, setting in motion events that would reshape the map of North America.
funFact: The US Army's use of horse artillery at Palo Alto was so effective it transformed military tactics across the continent.

name: Lake Travis
clue: This Highland Lakes reservoir on the Colorado River northwest of Austin is the playground of the Texas capital, stretching 65 miles through Hill Country canyons and providing flood control, water supply, and recreation.
funFact: Lake Travis can vary by over 100 feet in depth between flood and drought years due to its role as a flood-control reservoir.

name: Amistad National Recreation Area
clue: Straddling the US-Mexico border on the Rio Grande near Del Rio, this vast reservoir created by an international dam offers some of the best bass fishing in North America amid ancient desert canyon scenery.
funFact: The name 'Amistad' means friendship in Spanish, reflecting the international cooperation between the US and Mexico to build the dam.

name: Tyler
clue: Deep in the Piney Woods of East Texas, this city is known as the 'Rose Capital of America', producing roughly 20% of all commercially grown roses in the United States and hosting an annual rose festival since 1933.
funFact: The Tyler Municipal Rose Garden is the largest publicly owned rose garden in the US, with over 38,000 rose bushes of 500+ varieties.
